name: Kay Starr
abstract: Kay Starr (born Catherine Laverne Starks; July 21, 1922 – November 3, 2016) was an American singer who enjoyed considerable success in the late 1940s and 1950s. She was of Iroquois and Irish heritage.

concerts.label: Newport Jazz Festival: The Stardust Road: A Hoagy Carmichael Jubilee

works.t: In the Cool, Cool, Cool of the Evening

about_quotes.speaker: Red Callender
about_quotes.evidence: I did many things with her, including 'Sweet Lorraine,' 'Melancholy Baby,' 'Sweet Georgia Brown.' ... That was with Kay Starr; that was before she became a big name.

about_quotes.speaker: Bill Coleman
about_quotes.evidence: Kay Starr was a west coast singer that I had not heard about also. We recorded four numbers, and one included "Stormy Weather", which Kay sang

about_quotes.speaker: Charlie Barnet
about_quotes.evidence: I believe she replaced Kay Starr... And Kay sang with you what, then, in '46, or '45? Or '45, in there in that vicinity.

about_quotes.speaker: Gerald Wiggins
about_quotes.evidence: I’ve been with Lena Horne, Dinah Washington, Kay Starr, I even was with Dinah Shore one night.
about_quotes.doc_title: Gerald Wiggins interviewed by Monk Rowe, Los Angeles, California, September 2, 1995

about_quotes.speaker: Dick Hyman
about_quotes.evidence: And I also did an album with Basie for Kay Starr, who was a surprisingly hip singer and it was a surprisingly good album.
about_quotes.doc_title: Dick Hyman interviewed by Monk Rowe, Scottsdale, Arizona, March 4, 1995 [interview 1 of 2]

about_quotes.speaker: Patricia Willard
about_quotes.evidence: And the next group that came in to alternate with us, like our intermission group, was Kay Starr and her group.
